section.title: The River and the Borders of the Land
section.sectionRationale: After all the measured order of gates, chambers, and offerings, the vision suddenly overflows its own boundaries: water trickles from beneath the threshold and, without a single tributary joining it, becomes an uncrossable river that heals the Dead Sea itself and makes trees bear fruit every month. The chapter then widens further still, drawing borders for a land that will even include believing foreigners as full heirs.
section.thesis: Life flows out from the presence of God to heal the deadest places on earth and to make a home for anyone, native or foreigner, who will receive it, a river fulfilled in Christ's gift of living water and consummated in the river of life that flows from God's throne forever.
section.frameNote: Four scenes: the river measured deeper and deeper until it cannot be crossed; the river's life-giving and healing effect on the Dead Sea, its fish, and its fruit trees; the boundaries of the land the LORD gives as an inheritance; and the inclusion of resident foreigners as full heirs alongside the tribes of Israel.

verses.text: Then the man brought me back to the entrance of the temple, and I saw water flowing from under the threshold of the temple toward the east (for the temple faced east). The water was coming down from under the south side of the temple, south of the altar.
Next he brought me out through the north gate and led me around the outside to the outer gate facing east, and there I saw the water trickling out from the south side.
As the man went eastward with a measuring line in his hand, he measured off a thousand cubits and led me through ankle-deep water.
Then he measured off a thousand cubits and led me through knee-deep water. Again he measured a thousand cubits and led me through waist-deep water.
Once again he measured off a thousand cubits, but now it was a river that I could not cross, because the water had risen and was deep enough for swimming, a river that could not be crossed on foot.
“Son of man, do you see this?” he asked. Then he led me back to the bank of the river.
When I arrived, I saw a great number of trees along both banks of the river.

units.label: Everything will live where the river goes
units.note: the Dead Sea made fresh, trees for food and healing

verses.text: And he said to me, “This water flows out to the eastern region and goes down into the Arabah. When it empties into the Sea, the water there becomes fresh.

Wherever the river flows, there will be swarms of living creatures and a great number of fish, because it flows there and makes the waters fresh; so wherever the river flows, everything will flourish.
Fishermen will stand by the shore; from En-gedi to En-eglaim they will spread their nets to catch fish of many kinds, like the fish of the Great Sea.
But the swamps and marshes will not become fresh; they will be left for salt.

Along both banks of the river, fruit trees of all kinds will grow. Their leaves will not wither, and their fruit will not fail. Each month they will bear fruit, because the water from the sanctuary flows to them. Their fruit will be used for food and their leaves for {{marpe|healing}}.”
This is what the Lord GOD says: “These are the boundaries by which you are to divide the land as an inheritance among the twelve tribes of Israel; Joseph shall receive two portions.
You are to divide it equally among them. Because I swore with an uplifted hand to give it to your forefathers, this land will fall to you as an {{nachalah|inheritance}}.
This shall be the boundary of the land: On the north side it will extend from the Great Sea by way of Hethlon through Lebo-hamath to Zedad,
Berothah, and Sibraim (which is on the border between Damascus and Hamath), as far as Hazer-hatticon, which is on the border of Hauran.
So the border will run from the Sea to Hazar-enan, along the northern border of Damascus, with the territory of Hamath to the north. This will be the northern boundary.
On the east side the border will run between Hauran and Damascus, along the Jordan between Gilead and the land of Israel, to the Eastern Sea and as far as Tamar. This will be the eastern boundary.
On the south side it will run from Tamar to the waters of Meribath-kadesh, and along the Brook of Egypt to the Great Sea. This will be the southern boundary.
And on the west side, the Great Sea will be the boundary up to a point opposite Lebo-hamath. This will be the western boundary.

units.label: An inheritance for the foreigner too

verses.text: You are to divide this land among yourselves according to the tribes of Israel.

You shall allot it as an inheritance for yourselves and for the {{ger|foreigners}} who dwell among you and who have children. You are to treat them as native-born Israelites; along with you, they shall be allotted an inheritance among the tribes of Israel.
In whatever tribe a foreigner dwells, you are to assign his inheritance there,” declares the Lord GOD.

marpe.gloss: Healing, remedy, cure. The leaves of the river's trees are for marpe, healing, language Revelation 22:2 draws on directly for the healing of the nations by the tree of life.

nachalah.gloss: Inheritance, a possession given and secured by promise rather than earned. The land falls to Israel as a nachalah because the LORD swore it to their forefathers; the same word frames the priests' portion in chapter 44, where God Himself, not land, is their nachalah.

ger.gloss: Foreigner, sojourner, resident alien. Remarkably, the ger who dwells among Israel and has children is to be allotted an inheritance exactly as a native-born Israelite, a startling widening of belonging within the very same vision that elsewhere restricts entry to the sanctuary.

basic.context: This chapter is the high point of Ezekiel's entire temple vision. A trickle of water seeps from beneath the temple threshold, and as the measuring man leads Ezekiel eastward, it deepens with no tributary in sight, ankle, knee, waist, and finally a river too deep to cross. Wherever it flows it brings life: it sweetens the Dead Sea itself so fish teem in it, and it grows fruit trees on its banks that bear food every month and leaves for healing. The chapter then turns from water to land, tracing the borders of the inheritance the twelve tribes will divide, before closing with an extraordinary widening: foreigners who dwell among Israel and have children are to receive an inheritance exactly like the native-born, treated as full heirs rather than permanent outsiders.
basic.hermeneutics: The river's impossible growth, deepening without any tributary, is the clearest sign in the whole temple vision that this is theological picture rather than architectural plan; a river like this could not exist by ordinary hydrology. Its meaning is unmistakable regardless of how literally or symbolically the surrounding measurements are read: life flows from God's presence outward to heal what is deadest, and that life is offered even to the outsider.
basic.originalLanguages: The leaves of the trees are for marpe, healing, the very word and image John later draws on for the tree of life whose leaves heal the nations, Revelation 22:2. The land comes to Israel as a nachalah, an inheritance secured by God's own oath rather than earned, the identical word used of the priests' portion in chapter 44, where God Himself is their inheritance. And the ger, the resident foreigner, is here promised a nachalah of his own, treated as native-born rather than kept at the margins.
basic.typology: The river flowing from the temple, bringing life and healing wherever it goes, anticipates Jesus' promise of living water flowing from within those who believe in Him, John 7:37-39, and is consummated in the river of the water of life flowing from the throne of God and the Lamb, with the tree of life on its banks for the healing of the nations, Revelation 22:1-2. The foreigner's full inheritance anticipates the gospel's inclusion of the nations as fellow heirs with Israel in Christ.
basic.christ: On the last day of the feast, Jesus stood and cried out, 'If anyone is thirsty, let him come to Me and drink. Whoever believes in Me, as the Scripture has said, streams of living water will flow from within him,' and John explains this of the Spirit whom believers in Him were later to receive, John 7:37-39. Revelation 22:1-2 completes Ezekiel's picture exactly: a river of the water of life, bright as crystal, flowing from the throne of God and of the Lamb, with the tree of life on either side, bearing fruit every month, its leaves for the healing of the nations, the very language of Ezekiel 47:12 fulfilled and consummated.
basic.god: God is the source of all true life; nothing flows from anywhere else in this vision, only from beneath His own dwelling place. He is lavishly generous, sending a river that needs no tributary to become uncrossable, and He is astonishingly inclusive, granting the resident foreigner the very inheritance He swore to Israel's forefathers, a widening of grace that anticipates the gospel going out to all nations.
basic.crossReferences: John 7:37-39 records Jesus' invitation to come and drink of the living water that flows from within believers through the Spirit. Revelation 22:1-2 shows the river of the water of life and the tree of life whose leaves heal the nations, deliberately echoing Ezekiel 47:12. Zechariah 14:8 pictures living waters flowing out from Jerusalem, part of the same prophetic hope. Ephesians 2:12-13 and 2:19 describe Gentiles, once strangers to the covenants of promise, now fellow citizens with the saints, fulfilling the inclusion pictured in Ezekiel 47:22-23.
basic.oneStory: From a river flowing out of Eden to water the whole earth, Genesis 2:10, to a river flowing from a ruined and rebuilt temple to heal the Dead Sea, to a river flowing from the very throne of God and the Lamb at the end of all things, Scripture keeps returning to the same picture: God's presence is the source of all life, and that life flows outward without limit, healing what is deadest and gathering in even the outsider, until at last it reaches its full and final form in the new creation, where God's own dwelling with His people never runs dry.

context.literary: The theological high point of the entire temple vision, chapters 40-48, where the strict measurements and boundaries of the earlier chapters suddenly overflow into a river no measurement can contain, before the vision widens outward again into the land's borders and the foreigner's inheritance.
context.historical: Written to exiles who knew the Dead Sea as a place too salt and bitter to sustain life, this vision promises that very sea made fresh, and a land whose borders and inheritance would include even resident foreigners, an extraordinary hope for a scattered and humbled people.
context.canonical: Echoes Eden's river in Genesis 2:10 and Zechariah 14:8's living waters flowing from Jerusalem, while directly supplying the language John draws on for both Jesus' invitation to living water, John 7:37-39, and the river of the water of life in the new creation, Revelation 22:1-2.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To picture, in the most vivid terms available, the life-giving abundance that flows from God's restored presence, an abundance that heals what is deadest and includes even the foreigner as a full heir, so that Israel's hope would reach beyond mere political restoration to a renewed creation.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes a prophetic vision of a river and a future land inheritance; it is not a legislative code or literal engineering plan to be replicated. Its abiding truth is theological: life flows only from God's presence, and that life is offered generously, even to those outside the original covenant people.

debates.question: Is the river of Ezekiel 47 a literal future river in a rebuilt land, or a symbolic picture fulfilled in Christ and the new creation?

views.label: A literal future river
views.case: Some read this as describing an actual river that will flow from a rebuilt temple in a future, literal fulfillment, given the vision's careful, measured detail throughout chapters 40-48.

views.label: A symbolic picture fulfilled in Christ and consummated in Revelation
views.case: Others read the river's impossible, tributary-free growth as a clear signal of symbolic, theological picture, whose fulfillment is Christ's gift of the Spirit, John 7:37-39, and its consummation the river of life in Revelation 22:1-2, so that no future literal river is required or expected.

debates.bearing: Whichever view one holds about a future literal fulfillment, the New Testament's own use of this exact imagery for Christ and the new creation shows that the river's deepest meaning is already being fulfilled now, in the Spirit given to all who believe.

debates.question: How does the inclusion of foreigners as full heirs in 47:22-23 fit with the exclusion of uncircumcised foreigners from the sanctuary in 44:9?

views.label: Different concerns, land versus sanctuary access
views.case: The land inheritance in chapter 47 concerns civic belonging and provision, while the sanctuary restriction in chapter 44 concerns ritual access to the holy place itself; a foreigner could receive land while still needing genuine consecration to approach the altar.

views.label: A deliberate widening within the vision itself
views.case: Chapter 47's foreigner has children and has settled among Israel, suggesting genuine, ongoing devotion rather than mere ethnic presence, so the vision already anticipates that true belonging is a matter of committed faith, not bloodline, preparing for the gospel's fuller inclusion of the nations.

debates.bearing: Both readings affirm that this vision already stretches toward the gospel's insistence that belonging to God's people is finally a matter of faith and covenant loyalty, not ancestry alone, Romans 2:28-29.

study.originalLanguages: The trees' leaves are for marpe, healing, the very term and image behind the healing leaves of the tree of life in Revelation 22:2. Israel receives the land as a nachalah, an inheritance secured by God's own sworn oath rather than human achievement, the same word used for the priests' portion in God Himself, chapter 44:28. And the ger, the resident foreigner who has settled and had children among Israel, receives that same nachalah, treated as native-born rather than perpetual outsider.

typology.type: The river flowing from beneath the temple threshold, deepening without any tributary
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates Jesus' promise that streams of living water will flow from within all who believe in Him, John 7:37-39, the gift of the Spirit.

typology.type: The river's fruit trees bearing food every month and leaves for healing
typology.fulfillment: Consummated in Revelation 22:1-2, the river of the water of life and the tree of life whose leaves are for the healing of the nations.

typology.type: The foreigner granted a full inheritance alongside the tribes of Israel
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates Gentiles being made fellow citizens with the saints and members of God's household in Christ, Ephesians 2:19.

study.christ: On the last day of the feast, Jesus stood and cried out, 'If anyone is thirsty, let him come to Me and drink. Whoever believes in Me, as the Scripture has said, streams of living water will flow from within him,' and John tells us He spoke this of the Spirit whom believers in Him were later to receive, John 7:37-39. Revelation 22:1-2 then completes this vision exactly, showing the river of the water of life, bright as crystal, flowing from the throne of God and of the Lamb, with the tree of life on either side, bearing twelve kinds of fruit, yielding its fruit every month, and its leaves are for the healing of the nations, the very language of Ezekiel 47:12 brought to its final, cosmic fulfillment.
study.god: God alone is the source of all true life in this vision, water flows from nowhere else but beneath His own dwelling place, and it flows outward without limit or diminishment, needing no tributary because it is sustained entirely by His own presence. He is a God whose grace reaches further than expected, granting even the settled foreigner a full inheritance among His covenant people rather than leaving him a permanent outsider.

crossReferences.note: A river flows out of Eden to water the garden, the first appearance of the life-giving river imagery Ezekiel 47 and Revelation 22 both echo.
Jesus invites the thirsty to come and drink, promising streams of living water flowing from within believers through the Spirit.
The river of the water of life and the tree of life, its leaves for the healing of the nations, directly echoing Ezekiel 47:12.
Gentiles, once strangers to the covenants of promise, are made fellow citizens with the saints, fulfilling the foreigner's full inheritance pictured in Ezekiel 47:22-23.

study.oneStory: From the river flowing out of Eden to water the whole earth, Genesis 2:10, to this river flowing from a restored temple to heal the Dead Sea itself, to the river flowing from the very throne of God and the Lamb at the end of all things, Scripture returns again and again to one picture: life flows only from God's presence, and it flows outward without limit, healing what is deadest and welcoming in even the outsider. That river reaches its fullest form in Jesus, who offers living water to all who thirst, and in the new creation, where the river of life never runs dry and its leaves heal the nations forever.

quiz.q: What happens to the water's depth as the man measures it eastward from the temple, with no tributary joining it?

options.text: It deepens progressively from ankle-deep to knee-deep to waist-deep to an uncrossable river
options.note: 47:3-5 traces exactly this progression, deepening with no visible source but the temple itself.

options.text: It stays the same shallow depth the entire distance measured
options.note: The text explicitly describes continual deepening at each thousand-cubit measurement.

options.text: It grows shallower the farther it flows from the temple
options.note: The opposite occurs; the river deepens as it moves away from its source.

options.text: It splits into several smaller streams as it flows east
options.note: No splitting is described; it remains a single river that keeps deepening.

quiz.q: What effect does the river have on the Dead Sea, called here simply 'the Sea'?

options.text: The water there becomes fresh, teeming with swarms of living creatures and many fish
options.note: 47:8-9 describes this transformation of the notoriously lifeless Dead Sea.

options.text: It causes the Sea to dry up completely
options.note: The opposite happens; the Sea is filled with fresh, life-sustaining water.

options.text: It has no effect on the Sea at all, only on the land around it
options.note: 47:8 states directly that the water flowing into the Sea makes it fresh.

options.text: It turns the Sea into dry, arable farmland
options.note: The text describes the Sea becoming fresh water full of fish, not land.

quiz.q: What are the leaves of the river's fruit trees used for, according to 47:12?

options.text: Healing
options.note: 47:12 states plainly that the leaves are for healing, language Revelation 22:2 later echoes directly.

options.text: Weaving baskets for the temple
options.note: No such use is mentioned; the text specifies healing as the leaves' purpose.

options.text: Nothing; only the fruit is useful
options.note: The text explicitly gives the leaves a purpose distinct from the fruit, healing.

options.text: Fuel for the temple's sacrificial fires
options.note: The stated use of the leaves is healing, not fuel.

quiz.q: Who, in addition to native-born Israelites, is promised a full inheritance among the tribes in 47:22-23?

options.text: Foreigners who dwell among Israel and have children there, treated as native-born
options.note: 47:22 states this explicitly, an inheritance for these foreigners as if they were native Israelites.

options.text: No one else; the inheritance is strictly limited to the twelve tribes by blood
options.note: The text explicitly widens the inheritance to include settled foreigners as well.

options.text: Only the priests and Levites receive any additional portion here
options.note: This passage addresses foreigners specifically, not priestly allotments, which were fixed earlier in chapters 44-45.

options.text: Only foreigners who first renounce all family ties in their homeland
options.note: The text's condition is dwelling among Israel and having children there, not renouncing prior family ties.

quiz.q: How does Jesus' invitation in John 7:37-39 draw on the imagery of Ezekiel 47?

options.text: Jesus promises that streams of living water, referring to the Spirit, will flow from within all who believe in Him, echoing the river of life flowing from God's presence
options.note: John 7:37-39 records this invitation, which the New Testament and Revelation 22:1-2 tie directly to imagery like Ezekiel's river.

options.text: Jesus warns that no one may drink from the temple's water without priestly permission
options.note: This is the opposite of Jesus' open invitation, 'let him come to Me and drink.'

options.text: Jesus limits this promise only to the priests of Zadok's line
options.note: Jesus' invitation is addressed broadly, 'if anyone is thirsty,' not restricted to a priestly line.

options.text: Jesus reinterprets the river as referring only to physical, literal water for drinking
options.note: John clarifies that Jesus spoke of the Spirit, not merely literal water, John 7:39.

questions: The river deepens with no tributary in sight, growing entirely from what flows out of God's own presence. Where in your life have you tried to generate spiritual life on your own instead of receiving it as a gift flowing from God?

The river makes even the Dead Sea fresh and full of life. Is there a part of your life, a relationship, a habit, a wound, that feels as lifeless as the Dead Sea, that you need to bring under the flow of God's presence rather than trying to fix on your own?

The trees along the river bear fruit every month without fail, because the water never stops flowing from the sanctuary. What would change in your life if you drew on Christ, the living water, continually rather than only in moments of crisis?

The settled foreigner receives a full inheritance alongside native-born Israel. Who in your life might you be tempted to treat as a permanent outsider, and how does this vision, and the gospel it anticipates, call you to welcome them instead?

Revelation 22 shows this river finally reaching its full, unending form, with a tree whose leaves heal the nations. How does picturing that final scene shape the hope you carry into hard seasons of waiting right now?

translationNotes.note: An embedded line break in the source text between 'knee-deep water' and 'Again he measured' has been flattened into continuous prose here, matching the sentence's original sense.
'Deep enough for swimming' renders water too deep to wade through; the em dash before 'a river that could not be crossed on foot' has been rendered as a comma here without changing the meaning.
An embedded line break in the source text between 'This shall be the boundary of the land:' and 'On the north side' has been flattened into continuous prose here.
'Foreigners who dwell among you and who have children' reflects a settled, ongoing presence rather than a transient visitor, the basis on which this inheritance is granted.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session via the makor scripture toolkit for Ezekiel 47:1-23. The em dash at 47:5 replaced with a comma; embedded line breaks within 47:4 and 47:15 flattened into continuous prose. No em or en dashes used elsewhere. Web search this session (Truth For Life, Desiring God, Gospel Coalition, Gospel in Life) found no sermon from the trusted cluster (Begg, Keller, Piper, Ferguson, Carson) that specifically expounds Ezekiel 47; the Truth For Life page for this chapter lists only broad, multi-book overview messages (spanning Isaiah through Luke, or Ezra through Malachi), not a targeted exposition of this passage, so sermon status is none.
